provenience_detail: The tomb is known from relief-decorated fragments now held in the Egyptian Museum Cairo. C. M. Zivie suggested the tomb formed part of a now-lost New Kingdom cemetery at Giza. N. Staring has proposed the Teti Pyramid Cemetery at Saqqara, where it would have stood amongst those of Ptahmay’s peers, see: N. Staring, ‘The Memphite Necropolis through the Amarna Period: A Study of Private Patronage, Transmission of Iconographic Motifs, and Scene Details’, in F. Coppens (ed.), Continuity, Discontinuity and Change: Perspectives from the New Kingdom to the Roman Era, Prague, 2021, pp. 37–41.

provenience_detail:
The pyramidion of Ry was first thought to have come from Abydos. According to his own recollection, Auguste Mariette (1821–81) had excavated it there on the east slope of the central cemetery. Curiously, in the Journal d’entrée of the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, it is noted that the object was found “at Memphis”. The apparent mix-up has recently been cleared. Mariette’s unpublished inventaire, the precursor of the Journal d’entrée, lists the pyramidion among the finds he made at Saqqara in February 1861. In the late 1850s and early
1860s, Mariette engaged in excavations south of the Step Pyramid at Saqqara to gather a collection for the soon-to-be-opened Boulaq Museum.
The wall-fragment was originally installed on the north wall of the inner chapel of the tomb of Ry

provenience_detail: Seven fragments of it were recovered from the debris of the upper layers located to the southeast of the tomb; this took place during the 2007 excavation season, when these layers were being removed with a loader. It appears that this stela had already been broken into several pieces in antiquity and was scattered during the excavations carried out at the site since the end of the 19th century. It was found in the debris around the mudbrick enclosure wall of the temple of Anubis
